message:
style(wal): hoist mutex-lock take() out of `if let` and silence clippy::too_many_arguments

Two small follow-ups to the wal asupersync migration earlier in
this batch.

crates/fsqlite-wal/src/parallel_wal.rs

- start_on_runtime_with_fsync() and stop() previously nested a
  `.lock().unwrap_or_else(...).take()` chain directly inside an
  `if let Some(...) = { ... }` binding.  That binding holds the
  mutex guard alive across the entire body of the if-let arm
  because the temporary guard lives until the end of the
  enclosing expression.  The body inside these arms calls
  ticker_cx.cancel() / handle.wait(), both of which can block —
  blocking while holding the inner mutex guard would invite a
  deadlock if any other coordinator path tried to lock the same
  mutex in the meantime.
- Hoist the `.take()` expression into a `let prior_ticker_cx =
  ...;` / `let prior_handle = ...;` step that drops the mutex
  guard before the nested if-let runs, so by the time we call
  cancel() / wait() the lock is already released.
- Both hoists are identical in semantics to the old code under
  non-contended conditions; they only matter when another
  caller races to lock the same mutex.

- Add #[allow(clippy::too_many_arguments)] to epoch_ticker_loop.
  The asupersync migration grew the argument count from 6 to 8
  (adding loop_cx: Cx and keeping fsync_policy + flush_timeout).
  Packing them into a single struct would be gratuitous since
  the function has exactly one call site (the spawn_blocking
  closure in start_on_runtime_with_fsync), so the allow is the
  right call.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
